earth: This equatorial bulge makes the Earth's radius 43 kilometers larger at the equator compared to the poles, meaning the summit of Mount Chimborazo is actually the closest point on Earth to the Moon.
Convection currents in the outer core, driven by heat escape from the inner core, combined with the Coriolis effect, generate electric currents that create the global magnetosphere.
Known as the Lense-Thirring effect, this general relativistic warping causes a precession of orbiting gyroscopes, which was empirically validated to high precision.
Without the greenhouse effects of carbon dioxide, water vapor, and methane, Earth's average surface temperature would be a freezing -18°C instead of the comfortable 15°C.
jupiter: This powerful field is generated by electric currents in the swirling metallic hydrogen layer, creating intense auroras at its poles and a massive magnetosphere.
It is an anticyclone (high pressure system) that rotates counterclockwise once every six days. While shrinking in recent decades, it is still wider than the Earth.
Its immense gravity deflects incoming comets and asteroids, hurling them out of the solar system or consuming them, protecting Earth from catastrophic impacts.
This excess heat is generated by gravitational contraction (Kelvin-Helmholtz mechanism), meaning the planet is slowly shrinking by about 2 centimeters per year.
saturn: With a mean density of 0.687 g/cm³, Saturn is lighter than water, owing to its composition of light hydrogen and helium gases.
Despite spanning over 280,000 kilometers in width, the rings are paper-thin, disappearing from view when seen edge-on from Earth.
This hexagonal jet stream is about 30,000 kilometers wide, with winds blowing at 320 km/h, driven by complex atmospheric fluid dynamics.
Titan's atmosphere is mostly nitrogen with methane clouds, supporting liquid methane-ethane lakes and rain cycles on its freezing surface.

sagittarius-a: The image shows the shadow of the black hole's event horizon silhouetted against a glowing ring of superheated gas orbiting at near light-speed.
The star S2 orbits the black hole on a highly eccentric path, reaching 3% of the speed of light at pericenter, allowing scientists to verify gravitational redshift.
Sgr A* is starving, consuming only a tiny fraction of the gas surrounding it, which is why its accretion disk is faint compared to distant quasars.

andromeda-collision: Because of the vast empty distances between stars, the stars will simply drift past one another, though their orbits will be completely rearranged.
Often referred to as 'Milkydromeda', the resulting giant galaxy will lack the classic spiral arms, settling into a smooth spherical/elliptical shape.
As the galaxies approach, Andromeda will grow from a small smudge into a massive band of stars, gas, and active star-forming nebulae spanning the entire sky.
betelgeuse-supernova: For several weeks, the exploding star will shine as brightly as the full moon, casting shadows at night and visible during the day.
Depending on the final mass of the collapsing core, the remnant will compact into a spinning pulsar or collapse directly into a singularity.
Although it will release immense gamma rays and high-energy particles, the distance is far enough that Earth's atmosphere and magnetosphere will easily shield us.
